title: Guest post: How US renewable-energy growth persists despite federal policy uncertainty - Carbon Brief
gist: US renewable energy (solar, wind, battery storage) accounted for over 90% of new electricity capacity additions in both 2024 and 2025, driven by falling costs, IRA-era investments and state policies — persisting despite the current administration's pro-fossil-fuel pivot. Electricity demand is projected to rise 24-34% by 2035 from 2021 levels, creating a structural tailwind for renewables. The transition is now economically self-sustaining and crosses political lines, with Texas leading the nation in renewable capacity despite having no decarbonisation goals.

themes.theme: Renewable dominance in new capacity additions

insights.claim: Solar, wind and battery storage accounted for over 90% of new US electricity capacity in both 2024 and 2025, reaching 47 GW and 48 GW respectively in those years.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Renewables are structurally dominant in new US capacity build-out regardless of federal policy headwinds — a durable investment thesis.
insights.quote: additions of solar, wind and battery storage capacity reached more than 90% of total additions in 2024 and 2025 at 47 gigawatts (GW) and 48GW a year, respectively

insights.claim: Renewable energy capacity additions have outpaced every other electricity generation technology in the US since 2011, according to University of Maryland analysis of EIA data.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: The 15-year trend is unbroken — renewables have been the dominant incremental capacity source through multiple administrations.
insights.quote: capacity additions from renewable energy have outpaced that of every other technology since 2011, according to our research

insights.claim: 112 GW of US coal capacity was retired over the last decade due to market forces, health concerns and clean-energy policies, while gas-power additions remained at a low but steady level.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Coal retirement is irreversible at this scale; gas additions have not compensated, leaving a gap filled almost entirely by renewables.
insights.quote: 112GW of coal capacity was retired over the last decade due to market forces, health concerns and clean-energy policies

insights.claim: Solar generation increased nearly tenfold over the last decade in the US, while wind generation doubled; combined solar and wind reached 19% of total electricity generation in 2025.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Generation mix shift is accelerating — solar and wind together now rival coal's share (which fell more than 50% over the same period).
insights.quote: solar and wind reached 9% and 10% of the generation mix last year, respectively

insights.claim: Fossil-fired electricity as a whole has fallen to 56% of the US generation mix, down from a higher share a decade ago, while gas generation has risen to a 39% share.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: The US grid's fossil share is in structural decline; gas is the primary fossil beneficiary but its growth is constrained by renewables cost curves.
insights.quote: fossil-fired electricity as a whole has fallen to 56% of the mix

themes.theme: Electricity demand surge as structural tailwind

insights.claim: US electricity demand is projected to increase 24-34% in the next decade relative to 2021 levels, driven by data centres, transport electrification, buildings and industry.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: The demand growth inflection materially changes the investment case — renewables now need to serve both coal replacement and new load growth simultaneously.
insights.quote: US electricity demand could increase by 24-34% in the next decade, relative to 2021 levels
insights.locator: Rising electricity demand in the US section

insights.claim: US electricity demand grew 7% from 2020 to 2025 after two decades of stagnation, marking a clear inflection point driven by electrification and data centres.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: The end of flat demand removes the 'renewables only need to replace retirements' narrative — new build must exceed retirements, accelerating procurement.
insights.quote: demand has grown by 7% since 2020 – and this is set to accelerate
insights.locator: Rising electricity demand in the US section

insights.claim: Virginia, the US state with the highest number of data centres, passed new laws in 2025-2026 to allow more efficient grid utilisation and shift energy costs towards data centres while assisting low-income households.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Data centre energy costs are being legislatively socialised — a policy template other states may follow, affecting utility rate structures and renewable offtake.
insights.quote: Virginia – which has the highest number of data centres of any US state – just passed new laws to allow for more efficient grid utilisation and to shift energy costs towards data centres

themes.theme: Economics overtaking politics as renewable driver

insights.claim: Texas leads the US in renewable-energy expansion with nearly 90 GW of renewable electricity capacity in 2025, despite having no decarbonisation goals, driven by its deregulated power grid, lighter permitting and abundant resources.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Texas proves that economics, not ideology, is now the primary driver of US renewable buildout — red-state expansion is not contingent on federal climate policy.
insights.quote: Texas leads the nation in renewable-energy expansion, despite its lack of decarbonisation goals…increased renewable electricity capacity to nearly 90GW in 2025

insights.claim: Texas now generates more power from solar farms than from coal plants, a milestone achieved despite the state having no net-zero or decarbonisation goals.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Solar eclipsing coal in the largest fossil-energy state signals a structural, irreversible shift driven purely by economics.
insights.quote: The state now generates more power from solar farms than coal plants.

insights.claim: Between 2020 and 2025 declining costs became increasingly important renewable deployment drivers, contributing to a broader geographic dispersion of solar and wind across US states beyond early leaders like California and North Carolina.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Democratisation of renewable geography reduces concentration risk for investors and signals that the buildout is no longer dependent on a handful of pro-policy states.
insights.quote: declining costs and improving economics have become increasingly important drivers of renewable energy expansion, even amid increasing political and policy setbacks in some regions

insights.claim: Texas, Oklahoma and Iowa accounted for 62% of new US wind capacity over the last decade, while Texas, California and Florida built 52% of new solar capacity — geographic concentration driven by resource endowment.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: Transmission infrastructure linking resource-rich states to demand centres is a critical bottleneck and investment opportunity.
insights.quote: land- and wind-rich states such as Texas, Oklahoma and Iowa, have accounted for 62% of new wind capacity. Meanwhile, 'sun-belt' states such as Texas, California and Florida have built 52% of new solar capacity.

insights.claim: California's binding 100% renewable/zero-carbon electricity law drove renewable generation share to 44% in 2025, up from 16% a decade earlier, demonstrating the power of legislated targets.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: States with binding mandates show 3x faster renewable penetration — legislative strength of mandate is a key variable for investors in utility and project equity.
insights.quote: This has contributed to a 44% renewable generation share in the state in 2025, up from 16% only a decade ago.

insights.claim: New Mexico's renewable share reached over 50% of electricity generation by 2025, up from just 9% in 2015, driven by legislated 80% renewable by 2040 and 100% zero-carbon by 2045 targets.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: New Mexico's trajectory demonstrates that strong state mandates can drive rapid transformation even in moderate-resource states.
insights.quote: More than half of New Mexico's electricity is now generated by renewables, up from only 9% in 2015.

insights.claim: New Mexico's 3.5 GW SunZia wind and transmission project is set to be the largest renewable energy project in the western hemisphere when completed.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: The SunZia project signals that large-scale interstate transmission for renewables is now being built — overcoming one of the key structural barriers to renewable scaling.
insights.quote: The state's 3.5GW SunZia wind and transmission project is set to be the largest renewable energy project in the western hemisphere when completed.

insights.claim: Some US states are actively restricting climate action — Alabama has considered a solar-farm construction moratorium and Florida has banned net-zero policy and greenhouse-gas regulation — illustrating that partisan resistance remains a real risk.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: State-level regulatory risk is real in Republican-governed states and must be factored into project siting and permitting timelines.
insights.quote: Some states have tried to restrict climate action, spanning a potential solar-farm construction moratorium in Alabama to a ban on net-zero policy and greenhouse-gas regulation in Florida.

themes.theme: Fossil fuel legacy risks and IRA tailwinds

insights.claim: US coal production has fallen more than 40% over the last decade, tracking the decline in domestic coal consumption, while oil and gas production and exports have grown steadily since 2008.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: The fossil fuel mix is bifurcating — coal is in terminal decline while oil and gas benefit from continued demand and LNG export growth, creating distinct investment profiles.
insights.quote: Coal production has fallen more than 40% over the last decade, tracking the decline in domestic coal consumption

insights.claim: The US has become a net LNG exporter over the last decade, with oil and gas production and exports growing steadily since 2008, even as coal collapses domestically.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: US LNG export growth creates a structural link between domestic gas prices and global energy markets — a risk factor for gas-dependent power generators.
insights.quote: the US becoming a net liquified natural gas (LNG) exporter over the last decade

insights.claim: Investments spurred by the Inflation Reduction Act and Bipartisan Infrastructure Law remain a key driver of renewable growth, even as the current administration withdraws support for renewables at the federal level.
insights.speaker: Carbon Brief Staff
insights.so_what: IRA-committed capital is already deployed and creating durable demand — the policy reversal risk is significant for future cycles but less so for projects already under construction.
insights.quote: This is thanks to the falling cost of renewable energy technologies, investments spurred by the Inflation Reduction Act and Bipartisan Infrastructure Law and local and state policies
